query: laboratory findings in chronic kidney disease due to IgA nephropathy

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Despite being a common disease, accurate prevalence data for IgAN are limited due to the necessity of a renal biopsy to establish the diagnosis. Management entails inducing remission and preventing complications by addressing proteinuria, estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR), blood pressure, and histological findings, while treatment strategies aim to mitigate immune reactions, reduce IgA deposition, and slow renal damage progression. This activity comprehensively reviews the etiology, pathogenesis, evaluation, and management of IgAN while also explaining the role of the interprofessional healthcare team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Immunoglobulin A (IgA) nephropathy, or IgAN, also known as Berger disease, is one of the leading causes of glomerulonephritis and renal failure. This disease is a prevalent form of glomerulonephritis characterized by the deposition of IgA in the glomerular basement membrane. Immune-mediated damage to the basement membrane results in hematuria, proteinuria, and renal insufficiency. Pathologically, a spectrum of glomerular lesions may be seen, with mesangial proliferation and prominent IgA deposition being the most commonly observed change.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic hematuria with mild proteinuria, typically around 0.5 g/d, may be detected during routine screening. A subset of individuals presenting with isolated microscopic hematuria and mild proteinuria may eventually progress to develop substantial proteinuria and hypertension, underscoring the importance of long-term monitoring. [51] Progressive chronic kidney disease (CKD) is frequently observed in various cohorts. Renal survival rates exhibit significant variability depending on factors such as biopsy timing and the presence of lead-time bias. Estimated renal survival at the 10-year mark is thought to be between 57% and 91%. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical features and laboratory findings suggestive of IgA nephropathy. Implement evidence-based management strategies for IgA nephropathy, including pharmacological interventions, lifestyle modifications, and dietary recommendations. Assess the severity and progression of IgA nephropathy through regular monitoring of renal function, proteinuria, and other relevant parameters. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare providers to optimize IgA nephropathy management by selecting appropriate immunosuppressive agents and supportive therapies tailored to an individual's disease course and risk factors. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical course typically progresses gradually, yet between 20% and 50% of affected patients develop end-stage renal disease (ESRD) within 20 years of diagnosis. Prevalence varies based on ethnicity, race, geography, and genetics, with Asian patients being more commonly and severely affected. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation begins with establishing the diagnosis. The initial investigation involves urine analysis to detect microscopic hematuria. The presence of red cells and red cell casts indicates glomerular injury and potential active disease. Proteinuria is assessed using the protein-to-creatinine ratio in urine or 24-hour urinary protein excretion. Serum creatinine and estimated GFR are measured to quantify renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Etiology -- Sporadic or Idiopathic IgA Nephropathy
retrieved_docs.content: More than 90% of cases are sporadic. [10] No evidence suggests the involvement of a particular infectious agent despite the association between macroscopic hematuria and mucosal inflammation. No evidence indicates hypersensitivity to food antigens, except for a small group of patients with celiac disease. Abnormal O-glycosylation of the IgA1 hinge region promotes the formation of circulating IgA1-antibody immune complexes, leading to mesangial cell activation and deposition. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The management of IgAN begins with confirming the diagnosis, typically through a renal biopsy, while ruling out secondary causes. Key considerations for formulating the management plan include assessing proteinuria, eGFR, blood pressure, and histological findings. The primary goals of treatment are to induce remission and avert the onset of complications. [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin A -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: IgA nephropathy, also known as Berger disease, is a type of glomerulonephritis of the kidney. It is the most frequent cause of glomerulonephritis worldwide and has a multitude of clinical manifestations. [9] The hypothesis is that a deficiency in O–linked glycosylation of IgA1 results in immune complexes that trigger the glomerular injury. This condition results from deposits of IgA in the mesangium, a region of the glomerulus that is surrounded by capillaries.  On immunofluorescence, a granular pattern is visible due to IgA immune complex deposition. It often presents during childhood as hematuria with red blood cell cells and casts. An upper respiratory tract infection can precede it. IgA levels are increased in both serum and urine but are of no prognostic significance. The presentation can vary according to the age group affected. These individuals have mild proteinuria, and up to 50% may progress to renal failure depending on other confounding factors such as hypertension, decreased glomerular filtration rate, and proteinuria. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: During a physical examination, it is essential to assess blood pressure and observe for indications of diminished renal function, including edema, ascites, and basal lung crepitations. Hypertension is a common manifestation in IgAN. In addition, IgAN may coexist with cirrhosis, liver diseases, and celiac disease. Therefore, conducting a comprehensive general and abdominal examination is crucial for excluding these clinically significant comorbidities. The range of clinical features of IgAN is broad, ranging from asymptomatic hematuria to rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. The mode of presentation varies based on age group and histological biopsy patterns. Among the most prevalent clinical phenotypes are asymptomatic hematuria and progressive kidney disease.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Routine screening of hematuria in East Asian schoolchildren is believed to contribute to the early detection of microscopic hematuria, thereby contributing to the increased diagnosis of IgAN. However, data from dialysis and biopsy registries indicate a greater burden of IgAN in East and Pacific Asian countries. These findings align with geospatial differences in the prevalence of genetic susceptibility loci observed in global genome-wide association studies. [30] [31] [32] In addition, evidence also suggests that ESRD is more common among East Asian patients diagnosed with IgAN than non-Asian patients. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with sustained proteinuria of 1 g/d or higher have a 46-fold increased risk of ESRD than those with less than 500 mg/d. [73] About half of the patients with IgAN may obtain complete remission with treatment, whereas the remaining half may show some degree of ongoing disease. [73]

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although IgAN is the most common cause of glomerulonephritis, its exact prevalence remains uncertain due to many cases being asymptomatic and requiring a renal biopsy for definitive diagnosis. Not all patients undergo a biopsy to confirm the diagnosis, with some opting for conservative management, particularly if they exhibit a benign variant. Approximately 10% of renal biopsies in the United States reveal IgAN, while the prevalence is higher in East Asia (up to 40% of biopsies) and Europe (20% to 30% of biopsies). [4] [14] [28] A systematic analysis of biopsy-based literature across multiple countries indicates an overall incidence of over 2.5 per 100,000. [14] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical trajectory of IgAN typically spans a protracted course during which an interprofessional healthcare team collaboratively manages patient care. Initial detection often occurs through abnormal urinalysis findings during routine screening by primary care providers. Expedient referral to a nephrologist (or pediatric nephrologist) for comprehensive evaluation is imperative. Coexisting autoimmune diseases may necessitate input from rheumatologists, while systemic manifestations may require the expertise of endocrinologists, gastroenterologists, or other specialists. Moreover, patients should be provided with a "steroid card" for reference.

retrieved_docs.title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A proposed 4-hit model highlights abnormal mucosal IgA regulation as the central mechanism: Abnormal O-glycosylation of the IgA1 hinge region, leading to increased levels of galactose-deficient IgA1. Production of anti-glycan antibodies and IgA1 autoantibodies. Formation of IgA1 immune complexes circulating in the bloodstream and depositing in the kidney. Activation of mesangial cells, as well as the lectin and alternative complement systems, resulting in the release of cytokines, the deposition of extracellular proteins, and systemic inflammation and fibrosis. Other significant issues essential for navigating the diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis of IgAN are mentioned below. Distinguishing primary IgAN from secondary IgAN is crucial, as addressing the underlying disease is necessary for managing secondary cases. The lectin and alternative complement pathways are highly involved in IgAN pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a broad renal disorder that encompasses a spectrum of conditions including nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome. When elevated blood pressure causes progressive renal damage, the resulting condition is identified as chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, which is a specific subtype of chronic kidney disease. Recognizing that chronic kidney disease can arise from hypertension and that it includes various glomerular diseases such as nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome guides clinicians to prioritize blood‑pressure control and tailored management of the underlying renal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1438
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive renal condition that can arise from multiple specific etiologies. One recognized form is chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, which is classified as a subtype of chronic kidney disease. Another condition that is mapped to chronic kidney disease is glomerulonephritis/nephrosis (GLOMERULONEFRITIA/NEFROSIA), indicating that these inflammatory and scarring kidney disorders are considered manifestations of chronic kidney disease. In clinical data collection, the status of chronic kidney disease may also be recorded as “Unknown,” which is an accepted permissible value when the presence or cause of the disease cannot be determined. Recognizing these subtypes and permissible reporting options is important for accurate diagnosis, documentation, and management of patients with chronic kidney disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 969
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, a pattern that is also observed in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and in chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). Necrotizing glomerulonephritis represents a specific, severe form of glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) and is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), an autoimmune condition that manifests as renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). In addition, necrotizing glomerulonephritis is frequently associated with pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية), reflecting its systemic impact. Glomerulonephritis, in turn, is clinically associated with the development of chronic kidney failure, underscoring the progressive nature of renal injury in these disorders. Together, these relationships highlight how chronic inflammatory processes, autoimmune kidney diseases, and metabolic complications interrelate and contribute to long‑term renal impairment, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) and is directly linked to متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). In the broader context, التهاب كبيبات الكلى is clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure), indicating that patients with this type of glomerular inflammation are at risk for progressive loss of kidney function. Recognizing the relationship between necrotizing glomerulonephritis, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and the potential development of chronic renal failure is essential for timely diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2508
retrieved_docs.content: Urinary tract disorder (اضطراب السبيل البولي) encompasses a broad range of conditions affecting the urinary system, and within this category lies kidney failure (قصور الكلى). Kidney failure functions as a parent condition for specific renal diseases, including nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome. Understanding that nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome are subtypes of kidney failure, which itself is a subset of urinary tract disorders, provides clinicians with a hierarchical framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing these interrelated kidney and urinary system conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome” group is shown to have acute glomerulonephritis as a parent condition, underscoring the progression from acute inflammatory injury to chronic nephrotic states. Overall, these interconnected classifications emphasize that CKD resides within a network of renal pathologies ranging from acute glomerular inflammation to systemic immune‑mediated diseases such as lupus nephritis and thrombotic microangiopathies like hemolytic uremic syndrome. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in understanding disease progression, overlapping diagnostic categories, and the importance of integrated management across acute and chronic kidney disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a renal disorder that can arise from several etiologies and is associated with specific complications. Hypertension is a recognized cause, as the condition described as “Chronic kidney disease due to hypertension” is classified as a type of chronic kidney disease. Inflammatory and degenerative glomerular diseases such as glomerulonephritis and nephrosis are also mapped to chronic kidney disease, indicating that these conditions can progress to or be considered forms of CKD. A common complication of chronic kidney disease is anemia, reflected by the entity “Anemia in chronic kidney disease,” which is described as being due to the underlying renal impairment. For clinical documentation, the value “Unknown” is accepted as a permissible entry for the variable chronic kidney disease when the disease status cannot be determined. Understanding these cause‑effect relationships and documentation conventions supports accurate diagnosis, management, and record‑keeping for patients with chronic kidney disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, as both involve pathological changes in small arterial vessels that lead to elevated blood pressure and related end‑organ effects. Recognizing the overlap in their clinical presentations helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertension from secondary renal vascular disease, guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation, and informs management strategies aimed at controlling blood pressure and preserving kidney function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course. This chronic course can evolve into secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and also into chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). In addition, glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) is clinically associated with chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). These interconnections indicate that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease are at risk for metabolic complications such as secondary diabetes and subsequent renal impairment, and that glomerulonephritis may further contribute to chronic kidney dysfunction. Recognizing and monitoring these linked conditions are essential for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1361
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is classified within the broader category of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, reflecting its relationship to inflammatory and protein‑leakage kidney disorders. A specific subtype, chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, represents CKD that arises as a consequence of long‑standing high blood pressure, highlighting hypertension as an important etiologic factor for renal impairment. In clinical data collection, the entry “Unknown” is recognized as a permissible value for the CKD variable, allowing healthcare providers to record cases where the presence or cause of CKD cannot be definitively determined. Recognizing CKD’s placement under nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, its hypertension‑related form, and the allowance for an “Unknown” status supports accurate diagnosis, categorization, and documentation in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1033
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis, a form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (kidney glomerular disease), is associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease). The anti‑basement membrane disease commonly manifests as اختلال وظيفة الكلى (kidney dysfunction). Because necrotizing glomerulonephritis belongs to اعتلال كبيبات الكلى, the disease spectrum can progress to اليوريمية (uremia), a downstream complication of kidney glomerular pathology. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians identify underlying anti‑basement membrane mechanisms, anticipate renal functional impairment, and monitor for the development of uremia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2175
retrieved_docs.content: اختلال وظيفة الكلى is recognized as a clinical manifestation of داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيلي, indicating that anti‑basement membrane disease of the glomerulus commonly presents with renal functional impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a renal disorder that falls within the broader category of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, indicating its relationship to inflammatory and protein‑losing kidney conditions. A specific form of this disease, chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, is recognized as a subtype of chronic kidney disease, highlighting the role of sustained high blood pressure as an underlying cause. Among the clinical manifestations of chronic kidney disease is عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين, an intolerance to leucine‑containing proteins, which can contribute to the disease’s symptom profile and influence dietary management strategies. Understanding chronic kidney disease’s classification, its hypertensive subtype, and its protein intolerance manifestation is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

Together, these interconnections illustrate how hemodialysis, its acute and chronic complications, related comorbidities, specific glomerular pathologies, systemic autoimmune or drug‑induced syndromes, and targeted ultrasonographic evaluation of the urinary system are integral to understanding renal pathology and guiding comprehensive patient care.
